I think wabi-sabi comes from the tea ceremony, where simplicity and naturalness are important. The term 'wabi' might relate to a certain kind of rustic or austere beauty, while 'sabi' is about the beauty of things that have aged or are weathered. So together, they form an aesthetic that appreciates imperfection and the passage of time. But how does this connect to other Eastern philosophies? Maybe Buddhism? Because Buddhism emphasizes impermanence (mujō) and the acceptance of things as they are. That seems related. Also, Zen Buddhism might have influenced wabi-sabi, since Zen values simplicity and mindfulness.

What about other Japanese concepts? There's 'mono no aware,' which is the awareness of the impermanence of things. That's similar to wabi-sabi, but more about the emotional response to transience.
